chapter: Chapter 10: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

questions.question: What is the main function of the human eye?
questions.answer: The human eye enables us to see objects by detecting light and forming images on the retina.

questions.question: What is the retina?
questions.answer: The retina is a light-sensitive layer at the back of the eye where images are formed.

questions.question: What is the role of the iris in the human eye?
questions.answer: The iris controls the size of the pupil and regulates the amount of light entering the eye.

questions.question: What is the pupil?
questions.answer: The pupil is the small opening in the iris through which light enters the eye.

questions.question: What is the function of the eye lens?
questions.answer: The eye lens focuses light rays onto the retina to form a clear image.

questions.question: What is accommodation of the eye?
questions.answer: Accommodation is the ability of the eye lens to change its focal length to focus on objects at different distances.

questions.question: What is the near point of the human eye?
questions.answer: The near point is the closest distance at which an object can be seen clearly, usually about 25 cm.

questions.question: What is the far point of the human eye?
questions.answer: The far point is the farthest distance at which an object can be seen clearly, usually infinity for a normal eye.

questions.question: What is myopia?
questions.answer: Myopia is a defect of vision in which distant objects appear blurred while nearby objects are seen clearly.

questions.question: How is myopia corrected?
questions.answer: Myopia is corrected using a concave (diverging) lens.

questions.question: What is hypermetropia?
questions.answer: Hypermetropia is a defect of vision in which nearby objects appear blurred while distant objects are seen clearly.

questions.question: How is hypermetropia corrected?
questions.answer: Hypermetropia is corrected using a convex (converging) lens.

questions.question: What is presbyopia?
questions.answer: Presbyopia is an age-related defect in which the eye loses its ability to focus on nearby objects.

questions.question: What causes presbyopia?
questions.answer: Presbyopia occurs due to weakening of eye muscles and loss of flexibility of the eye lens with age.

questions.question: What is dispersion of light?
questions.answer: Dispersion is the splitting of white light into its constituent colours when it passes through a prism.

questions.question: What is the spectrum?
questions.answer: The spectrum is the band of seven colours produced when white light is dispersed.

questions.question: Name the colours of the visible spectrum.
questions.answer: Violet, Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange, and Red (VIBGYOR).

questions.question: What is scattering of light?
questions.answer: Scattering of light is the deviation of light in different directions when it interacts with particles in the atmosphere.

questions.question: Why does the sky appear blue?
questions.answer: The sky appears blue because shorter wavelengths of light (blue) are scattered more by the atmosphere.

questions.question: Why does the Sun appear reddish during sunrise and sunset?
questions.answer: During sunrise and sunset, sunlight travels a longer distance through the atmosphere, scattering shorter wavelengths and leaving red light.

questions.question: What is the function of the optic nerve?
questions.answer: The optic nerve carries visual information from the retina to the brain.

questions.question: What are rods and cones?
questions.answer: Rods and cones are photoreceptor cells in the retina that detect light and colour.

questions.question: What is colour blindness?
questions.answer: Colour blindness is a condition where a person cannot distinguish certain colours due to defective cone cells.

questions.question: What happens to light when it passes through a prism?
questions.answer: White light splits into its component colours due to dispersion.

questions.question: What role does the brain play in vision?
questions.answer: The brain interprets signals from the optic nerve to produce the images we see.
